proposals.target_probe: P16-longform (beaten by jalaalrd 9.75 vs 8.75, margin 1.0; and humanize 9.25, margin 0.50) and P19-social (beaten by humanize & slopless, both 10.0 vs 9.5, margin 0.50)
proposals.mechanism: Both losses have the identical root cause named by the judges: no-slop's own output carried more than one triad because the protocol counts triads locally within a sentence but never forces a whole-document tally. The failing pieces put one triad in the opening ('a date, a scope, and consequences'), one mid-body ('the interrupts, the shared-service dependency, the review queue'), and a third in the closer ('make the uncertainty visible, count the work nobody mentions, and refuse...'). Each read as 'the one allowed triad' in isolation. The fix is to make the allowance a document-wide COUNT, not a per-instance judgment — a countable unit, which the task notes have historically bound, unlike the current soft 'one, only where...' phrasing.
proposals.anchor: A four-item series is a hit too: a triad that failed to stop.
proposals.insertion: Count across the whole piece, not sentence by sentence: opening, body, and closer each hide their own, and every one reads like the single permitted one until you tally them together. Run a tab, one mark per triad, before you decide which single instance keeps the allowance.
proposals.why: The judges' c3/c5 evidence is consistent across five excerpts and two probes: the breach is always the SECOND and THIRD triad slipping past because each looks locally legal. The current text ('Allowance: one') names a count but the reader applies it per-sentence; forcing a running tab across opening/body/closer converts a declarative one-triad rule into an enforced count. This single edit addresses the dominant weakness in both P16 (margin up to 1.0) and P19 (margin 0.50), so it is the highest-leverage change even though P19 alone sits at the noise floor.

verdict.reasoning:
ANCHOR verifies: "A four-item series is a hit too: a triad that failed to stop." appears at line 167, verbatim and uniquely (single occurrence in the file).

ADDS BEHAVIOR (thin but real): The doctrine already establishes whole-deliverable counting for triads — line 167 "Two clean triads already fails" and C11 line 119 "more than one list-of-three per prose deliverable is rhythm, not count." So the *count-across-the-whole-piece* idea is NOT new. What is new is the enforcement mechanism: a running tab, one mark per triad, keyed to structural position (opening/body/closer), decided as you go rather than after. That procedural "how you catch the 2nd and 3rd" is not spelled out anywhere in the current protocol, which only names the allowance and the fail threshold. This targets exactly the judges' c3/c5 evidence that the breach is the second/third triad slipping past because each looks locally legal.

NO CONTRADICTION: reinforces "Allowance: one" and "Two clean triads already fails"; consistent with the counting-replaces-feeling frame (line 165, "Counting replaces feeling").

PLAUSIBLY FLIPS TARGET: converting the declarative allowance into an enforced running tally is a genuine tightening; the WHY itself concedes P19 sits at the noise floor and the leverage is mostly on P16. Clears the bar, but marginally.

IMPROVE (required): the proposed insertion is wordy and self-incriminating. "opening, body, and closer" is itself a list of three embedded inside the rule that polices lists of three — a direct hit on its own allowance. It also largely restates "Two clean triads already fails." Revised version below strips the duplication, removes the self-triad, and keeps only the load-bearing mechanism (running tab, mark as you go, count is per-deliverable not per-sentence).
verdict.revised_insertion: The count is per deliverable, not per sentence: keep a running tab from the first word, one mark each time a triad lands, so the second and third — each locally legal — are caught by the tally rather than by feel. Decide which single instance keeps the allowance only after the whole piece is marked.

proposals.mechanism: The pivot allowance is already zero, but the judges flagged pivots the model didn't recognize as pivots: cross-sentence negation-then-assertion ('This is not lying...; it is how planning works.' and 'The manager's job is not to extract tighter estimates. It is to...'). The current enumeration names 'not-just-X-but-Y, not-X-it's-Y, rather than just, and strawman reframe' — all of which read as SINGLE-clause or within-sentence templates. The distributed 'not X [full stop] It is Y' form escapes because the negation and the assertion sit in two separate sentences. Extending the named family to the split-sentence form closes the gap.
proposals.anchor: Count every not-just-X-but-Y, not-X-it's-Y, "rather than just," and strawman reframe — the whole contrast family, not only the "but" form.
proposals.insertion: The split form counts too: a negated sentence answered by an asserting one across a period ("This is not X. It is Y." / "The job is not to A. It is to B.") is the same pivot wearing two sentences; the period does not launder it.
proposals.why: Both P16 judges named this exact construction verbatim as the pivot that breached an allowance-of-zero criterion. The model isn't ignoring the ban — it doesn't classify the two-sentence form as a member of the contrast family, so it never counts it. Naming the split-sentence variant inside the already-binding count is the minimal fix, and the margin to jalaalrd (1.0) makes this a real loss, not noise.

verdict.reasoning:
Anchor exists verbatim and uniquely: line 168, the P2 "Pivots" rule ("Count every not-just-X-but-Y, not-X-it's-Y, 'rather than just,' and strawman reframe — the whole contrast family, not only the 'but' form"). The bare token "not-just-X-but-Y" recurs in C11 (line 119), but the full anchor sentence is unique to line 168, so the insertion point is unambiguous.

Adds behavior the doctrine lacks: every currently-listed pivot member is a single-sentence construction (X-but-Y, X-it's-Y, "rather than just," strawman reframe). The two-sentence split form — a negated sentence answered across a period by an asserting one ("This is not X. It is Y.") — is not a member of any listed form, so a model reading "the whole contrast family" can plausibly exclude it precisely because the negation and assertion are separated by a period rather than joined. Naming it closes a real gap.

No contradiction: the allowance is already zero and the fix ("state the assertion, drop the negated half") already applies; the insertion only expands membership of the already-banned family, fully consistent with the rule.

Plausibly flips the target cell: the WHY reports both P16 judges naming this exact split-sentence construction verbatim as the breach of a zero-allowance criterion, with a real margin (1.0 to jalaalrd). Adding the split form to the already-binding count is the minimal change that would make the model count and rewrite it, so it plausibly flips the cell rather than being noise.

Improvement: tightened the wording. The original's "answered by an asserting one across a period," the dual examples, and the trailing clause ran long; I kept both illustrative examples and the operative "the period does not launder it" instruction while cutting redundancy.
verdict.revised_insertion: The split form counts too: a negated sentence answered across a period by an asserting one ("This is not X. It is Y." / "The job is not to A. It is to B.") is the same pivot wearing two sentences — the period does not launder it.

proposals.target_probe: P18-empathy (beaten by bare 9.0 and slopless 9.0 vs 8.75, margin 0.25 each)
proposals.mechanism: Every one of the five judge excerpts names the same defect: writing to a panicking human, no-slop reached for bold run-in pseudo-headers ('What to actually do, in order:', "Two things I can't do responsibly:") and a full five-item numbered list with sub-points. C7 bans run-in bold labels and caps lists generally, and C9 says plain-is-not-cold, but nothing ties the DISTRESS register to collapsing scaffolding entirely. The winners shipped flatter prose. The fix names the countable trigger: when the reader is in acute distress, the structure ceiling drops to zero headers and at most one short list. A register-scoped count, not another declarative 'be conversational.'
proposals.anchor: bad news to a distressed user as bare bullets is performing detachment (C5).
proposals.insertion: The distress register also caps structure hard: to someone acutely frightened or panicking, zero headers and zero run-in bold labels, at most one short list, and the human sentence lands before the first instruction — a numbered action-stack with bolded lead-ins reads as a compliance memo, which is its own performed detachment.
proposals.why: The 0.25 margin is below the 0.5 noise floor, but the evidence is not noise: five separate judge excerpts across two arms name the identical failure (bold pseudo-headers + five-item list + action-before-acknowledgment). That cross-judge, cross-arm consistency is signal. C9 is where distress register already lives, so anchoring the structure-cap and the acknowledgment-ordering rule there scopes it precisely to the empathy case without touching the general structure rules in C7. The rule names countable units (zero headers, at most one list, acknowledgment before first instruction), the form that has historically bound.

verdict.reasoning: Anchor "bad news to a distressed user as bare bullets is performing detachment (C5)" exists verbatim and uniquely at line 115, the closing sentence of C9 — the correct home, since C9 is where the distress register already lives. The insertion adds behavior the doctrine lacks: (1) an acknowledgment-before-instruction ordering rule ("the human sentence lands before the first instruction"), which sharpens C9's looser "one true human sentence, then substance" into a countable ordering constraint and frames action-first as its own performed detachment; (2) a distress-specific structure cap that overrides C7's escape hatch — C7 caps structure only "unless the user asked for structure," whereas acute fear caps it regardless. No contradiction: it tightens C7 for the empathy sub-case rather than opposing it, and stays consistent with C5 and C9. It plausibly flips the target cell — the WHY reports five cross-judge, cross-arm excerpts naming the identical failure (bold pseudo-headers + five-item list + action-before-acknowledgment), and the insertion names exactly those countable units (zero headers, zero run-in bold labels, at most one short list, acknowledgment before first instruction), which is the historically binding form. Improved for wordiness and em-dash density (original had three em-dashes; the prose protocol caps at two per 200 words and the sentence is short).
verdict.revised_insertion: The distress register also caps structure hard. To someone acutely frightened or panicking: zero headers, zero run-in bold labels, at most one short list, and the human sentence lands before the first instruction. A numbered action-stack with bolded lead-ins reads as a compliance memo, which is its own performed detachment.
